speaker: interviewer
text: And rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Date, January 4, 1996. Survivor, Elsbeth Lewin, maiden name Gaertner. Interviewer, Marlene Glassman. City/state, Princeton, New Jersey. Country, USA. And this interview is being conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Marlene Glassman. Today I have the privilege of interviewing Mrs. Elsbeth Lewin. Today's date is January 4, 1996. And this interview is being conducted in Princeton, New Jersey, USA. OK. I'd like to begin by asking you to please state your full name and spell it for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Elsbeth Lewin. E-L-S-B-E-T-H L-E-W-I-N. My maiden name was Gaertner-- G-A-E-R-T-N-E-R. I was born in Mainz, Germany, on the Rhine. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Mainz for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-I-N-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names, any-- a Hebrew name, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- my Hebrew name was Sprinzel, which is really Western Yiddish. Comes from the Spanish Esperanza. And it was my great-great-grandmother's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Sprinzel for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-P-R-I-N-Z-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us what your maiden name was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name was Gaertner. G-A-E-R-T-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your date of birth--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have any other names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on July 4, 1924, in Mainz, in the-- in the-- in the hospital there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- your current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: My current age is 71.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you a little bit about Mainz. Where exactly is Mainz located?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainz is located, as I said, on the Rhine, right opposite where the river Main, M-I-N, flows into the Rhine. Main is sort of an industrial river in its last-- in-- in its last stages. And then the Rhine, the-- the-- the-- the Rhine makes a bend where Mainz is. And then it-- And Mainz is located right in the middle of the wine country. South of it are the wines of the Rheinhessen, Nierstein, Oppenheim. North of it, or rather, and-- and west, is the so-called Rheingau, where the Rieslings, the Johannisberger, and so on, come from. And my family was in the wine business for generations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Take us down the streets of [? Mainz. ?] What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an old town, which-- with a-- dominated by a cathedral, the outlines of-- of a-- of an ancient cathedral in which the electors were crowned in the Middle Ages and beyond. It's a Roman settlement. And we assume, safely assume, that the Jews came with the Romans, possibly as slaves, possibly as free men, as traders. But they came with-- as, you know, around, who knows. One-- one-- Anno Domini 1. And it's still an old town. It's-- it was heavily, heavily bombed in World War II. And this has-- it's-- some-- some of it was precision bombing, around the old cathedral. The Americans and English had good maps. And in fact, they left a rather large part of the old town standing, in order to protect the cathedral. So that is still there. But much of the bombed-out parts are still being rebuilt. And they are-- much of it is now pedestrian areas, so that the center of town is no longer accessible by cars. It's all around it. So then the-- then the newer part, where we lived, which was only settled in the 1800s, broad boulevards, pleasant streets, but mostly apartment-- low apartment houses. There are a lot of private homes also in the areas that are a little more outlying, expensive areas.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to talk a little bit about the Jewish population of Mainz. How many Jewish people would you say lived in Mainz before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around between 2,000 and 3,000, in a-- in a town of a population of about 130,000. And as I said, the-- the community was a very old one, which had periodically been destroyed. They have a timeline in the museum there which showed that there were times when it dwindled down to almost nothing. Particularly in 1096, the time of the First Crusade, which had enormous repercussions and the community was almost wiped out. And as a matter of fact, when-- if you remember your Yom Kippur service, there is-- in-- in the-- in the Machzor, there is a-- a space of all the-- all the places where there were martyrs in the martyrology. And Mayence, which is the French name for Mainz, is one of them. Also, in the Yom Kippur service and in the-- in the Rosh Hashanah service, there is a very important prayer, the Unesanneh [? Tokef. ?] We called it [? 'tao-kef.' ?] It's Unesanneh Tokef, which was written by one of the martyrs in Mainz just before he died. And it has remained in-- in-- in the service ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did Jews just before the war contribute to-- to the life of Mainz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were merchants as my family were. There were lawyers, there were doctors, there were artists. [INAUDIBLE], I'm not sure how Jewish he was. But he was a-- a-- a-- a-- in Germany, a famous novelist. Anna Seghers, who's also a novelist, was also from Mainz. Both of them, of course, left Germany, or had to leave. There were people of-- of all callings. There were also people who were, you know, just workmen. I mean, you know, ordinary people made-- made-- made-- made a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your dad. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was, in German, it was Willi Ernst Gaertner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-I-L-I E-R-N-- E-- wait a minute. E-R-N-S-T G-A-E-R-T-N-E-R. He was-- well, do you want me to go way back?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I'd like you to tell-- tell us his birthdate, if you would.

speaker: interviewee
text: His birthday was March 2, 1891. He was not born in Mainz. He was born in a town in the Rhineland called Julich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-U-- with an umlaut-- L-I-C-H. And later on, their family moved to Solingen, S-O-L-I-N-G-E-N. And he had two brothers, both of whom were killed in the First World War. And after he came-- he also was in the First World War. In fact, he was one of-- he was a driver of a-- of a truck, a truck driver, in-- that was very new in those days. And he had been trained to do that. And after he came out of the war, he never touched the wheel of a car again. Ever. Of course, he lived in New York. He didn't have to drive. But anyway-- and-- and-- but he should-- he could have driven when he was traveling. He was a wine merchant. And in those days, and perhaps still now, the wine merchants-- certainly in his type of business-- visited people. They went on long trips. And it was, you know, hours and hours by train. And of course, you didn't fly in those days. And they stayed in the area and announced themselves that they would like to call on a customer, a private customer, privately. Made a social call-- it was practically a social call, with gloves and spats and-- and-- and-- and a hat, and so on-- and were received in a social way. And then presented their-- well, it was almost like a menu, their-- their-- their-- their-- their their card of what they had to offer. And the customer would order by the-- by the case of-- of wine. And, of course, if it was an established customer, they would trust the taste of the business. If not, maybe they knew somebody else who was an established customer. So he had a steady clientele. But he was away for, oh, three, four, five weeks at a go. Because they would travel to East Prussia, and-- my grandfather did this too-- and sell there. And they would be in touch by phone. Every Friday night, they both called up, and-- because we were together with my grandparents, and-- and, you know, we were all together. And we all got on the phone. And it wasn't that far in the dark ages that there was no phone. But he-- it was a sort of a-- a gentlemanly profession. And he got along with all these people who were, oh, landed gentry and what have you. But also, doctors and lawyers and so on-- people who love to drink wine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was he in this business? Did this-- did his father have this business, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they moved to-- after the war, after the First World War, when his two brothers were killed, my grandparents didn't want to stay where they were. There were too many memories. And they moved to Mainz, where they had a lot of relatives. My-- my grandmother had a lot of relatives there. And my grandfather got into business, first with his brother-in-law, my mother's-- my grandmother's brother, and then with somebody else who had an established wine business. And they-- so my father and my grandfathers were partners. And they stayed in this-- this was, you know, in the '20s, early '20s. And they stayed in this business until 1938 when they were forced by the government to give up the business. And they sold it to one of their employees who had connections in the party. And the-- whatever the proceeds of the sale were, were very low. And my father was in touch with the man who bought it after the war. But he didn't want it back. I mean, obviously he was here, and he did-- he-- he was-- he was not-- no longer interested in retrieving it. But he was interested in how it was going. And I don't know what happened to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think this was in 1928. So this was even--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It-- it-- it was-- was--

speaker: interviewer
text: 1938? When-- when did they turn over the business? Because you said they weren't allowed to have-- have a business.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the late '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in the late '30s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: As-- it-- was that as a result of the Nuremberg laws? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, it was a combination of the Nuremberg laws, and-- and then later on, there were laws when-- when Jews weren't allowed to be in business at all anymore. And also, the German population was not allowed to-- to-- to buy from Jews. I mean, this-- you know, it was a two-way street. In fact, my father and my grandfather had seen this coming. And they started visiting Holland, the Netherlands, and had a good clientele there. So they were able to carry this business on for quite a while. But they were sort of in touch with all kinds of things before Hitler came to power. And when the Rhineland became free of the French occupation, my-- the-- the firm sent a case of wine to then-president Hindenburg. And we have a letter, which I can show you, thanking them for this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, they were-- they were contacted by whatever his-- Schacht, who was a high official in the Hitler regime, in 1938-- I have the letter for that too-- said he had drunk a glass of wine at one of his friends' and-- who was their customer-- and do they still have the same wine? He'd like to buy some. He didn't know it was a Jewish firm. And so this was-- and I suppose they sold it to him. I don't know. [LAUGHS] But-- Tell me about your mom. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: P my mother's maiden-- my mother's maiden name was Oppenheimer, O-P-P-E-N-H-E-I-M-E-R. Her name was Johanna and she was called Hanna. She had been born in a-- a small town named Camberg, which is, oh, not very far from there. It's in the Taunus--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-A-M-B-E-R-G. It's in the Taunus mountains across the Rhine. And her father had also been in trade. He had been in-- in the grain trade in this small town. They had lived in this small town for generations. It was a town of maybe 2,000 or 3,000 people, with a Jewish congregation. And my grandmother was very active, also, in the First World War. She was knitting and-- you know, with the local women. There was a whole group of supporters. And I will tell you about my-- my other grandmother later. My mother's-- my mother, after she married, she didn't work, until sort of the-- the business needed some family support. And-- and so she helped in the business. She was trained during the First World War as a volunteer nurse. And so when we had-- we saw that eventually we'd have to emigrate, she took a refresher course. And in fact, when we came to this country, she was-- she-- she took an exam, in a language she barely knew, and started working as a private-duty nurse. She later stopped that and, you know, did other things, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the exact address of where you lived in Mainz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived at a number of addresses. But the one I can remember best was Feldberg Platz [GERMAN], that-- it is 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-E-L-D-B-E-R-G P-L-A-T-Z. And Feldberg was a mountain in the Taunus range, which was quite a bit away. It was only 800 meters high. But on a clear day, we could look out of our window on the Feldberg Platz and see the Feldberg across the Rhine. It's become rather hazy now and I don't think one can see this very often anymore. But we could look from our balcony and see the-- and that's why it was called Feldberg Platz. It was the place where you could see the Feldberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that-- that was the first location? Or was that-- what was the most--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the one I know, I remember best, because we lived there from 1930 to 1937. Before, we had lived in the Kaiser Strasse, which was the big boulevard. And afterwards, we lived on the Kaiser Strasse again, but it-- we had to move out of this beautiful house of which I will show you a-- a picture. We-- because it was owned by non-Jews and we had to move into a house that was owned by Jews. And so we moved into-- it was not-- not very far away. And it was not a bad apartment, either. But this particular apartment was spectacular, in a spectacular house. And it's the sort of thing that when you are, you know, this was-- that-- that was my childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, describe that--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the time I was six--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --till the time--

speaker: interviewer
text: --spectacular about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as-- you-- when I show to you the-- the picture of the house, you will see it's a spectacular house. But the apartment, you-- you walked up two flights. And you came into a big open room, which was called a [? diele. ?] It was a foyer kind of a thing. But it was large enough to contain the ping pong table when I wanted to put the ping pong table up. And you could play, you know, on either end. And-- and it-- and off that one that was the central room, there were the other rooms. And there was an enormous living room, and a dining room, out of which there was a balcony, which had this view over the Rhine and over the Feldberg. And I had the room next to that. And one side went out with that same view over the Rhine, and-- where I had sort of a little nook with my books, and my desk, and everything. And the other side went out over the Feldberg Platz. And, oh, was my-- my bed, and my clothes, and stuff like that. I mean, well, you know. We did live ordinary lives. We did have to go to sleep. [LAUGHS] And, you know, there was kitchen, bathrooms, what have you. But it was just an unusual place. I mean, it was more unusual than my friends' apartments, most of my friends'-- which were nice. But, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived in this apartment with your mother, your father, and you. Did you have any other help in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had help in the house until the Nuremberg Laws cut that off. And after that, we had sort of young relatives who wanted to learn household. But they lived in the attic. I mean, as in all European places, the help lives in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the help?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one called Lena. She's the only one I can remember. I-- there may have been others, but I don't remember them very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to talk a little bit about your education in Mainz. Did you have a formal Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, depends what you call formal Jewish. In-- in Germany, there is a requirement that all school children take a-- a-- a religious education. And this still holds, incidentally. I was, for four years, in the-- in primary school. This is-- that's the-- the-- the-- the German way. And-- and-- and I was in a-- a regular primary school from 1930 to-- this was a-- a municipal school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Called Schiller Schule, after the poet, Schiller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you Spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-C-H-I-L-L-E-R Schule. And we had the same teacher throughout four years. [? In-- ?] in fact, she may have stayed on and taught them even-- taught the-- the people who remained in that particular school even longer. Because they were very attached to her. I've recently heard from some-- some of the-- my former classmates, whom I can't remember, incidentally. But then, in 1934, the usual thing is if you wanted to go and have an academic education, you would change over to a gymnasium or something of the sort. And in-- in Germany, in this case, it was called [GERMAN], [? higher ?] daughters' school. I mean, you know, not higher daughters, but school-- higher-- higher school for daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an all-- all-girls' school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an all-girls' school at the time. And I was required by the authorities to take an entrance examination there, even though my grades did not warrant an entrance examination. And my parents felt immediately that this was put in for a Jewish child. And when I came out of the-- And-- and at the same time, I should say, with the knowledge that it was probably not going to be possible for Jewish children to attend the public schools, a Jewish district school had been formed. There had always been an old orthodox Jewish school, because there was an old orthodox community. But I-- there was no thought-- was ever given to my going there, because it was orthodox. Even though I know some of the people who were in it. And-- and-- but at the time, in 1934, they formed this new school. And the staff of the new school consisted of teachers, Jewish teachers, who had been let go by the municipal schools, by the-- the public schools, who were no longer allowed to teach there. Consequently-- and they were brilliant teachers. Consequently, this school was organized to take all grades. And, you know, it was large enough to-- to have-- you know. There were enough children to have a substantial school. And fortunately, there was also a space for it, near the synagogue. The-- the synagogue, which had been built in 1912, had-- had some extra rooms for meeting rooms and so on. So there was space for it. So we had a very nice school with excellent teachers. And [? I-- ?] I must come back for a minute. When I came out of that exam, and my parents realized what they put me through unnecessarily, they said, you're not going to go there. You're going to the Jewish district school. And I went there and had my teachers became lifelong friends. The-- my fellow students are lifelong friends. We were a small community and we were not allowed to be in touch with anybody outside. I mean, nobody-- you know. It was-- it was not possible to be friends with non-Jewish children. So we were very close. And we did everything together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- last-- two years ago, we met again in Mainz. The city invited us-- the Jew-- former Jewish-- former inhabitants of the Jewish faith. They've been doing that for four years now. And they invited most of our class, our year, together. I must say I pushed them a little on that. And it was-- those of us who were still alive were extremely happy to see each other. So you-- you attended this school and you went to a Jewish gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it-- it was-- this school was a school that went all the way through. Whatever, first grade through-- what-- what-- what was in-- in Germany was the-- the last grade, [? prima. ?] And-- and they-- and they-- did-- the-- the-- the exam for that. But I went from the fifth grade on, till 1938, when the synagogue was first burned and then dynamited. And of course, there was no-- no going there anymore. And-- and for the rest of my stay in Germany, we were sort of, oh, an empty apartment here, an empty room there, or, you know. It was-- it was, well, no longer the school that we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.
